phases.name: Part 1 of 3
phases.title: Before You Press Play
phases.sub: Research the context first. Knowing WHY this match is famous transforms watching into understanding.

sections.title: Identify the Match & Its Place in History

items.q: What format is this? Test, ODI, or T20? Which series/tournament?
items.d: The format entirely changes what you're watching for. A Test is 5 days of attrition. A T20 is 3 hours of chess.

items.q: What year is this match from? What era of cricket does it represent?
items.d: 1970s cricket = pace dominance, uncovered pitches. 1990s = golden age of batting. 2000s+ = T20 revolution begins.

items.q: Why is this match famous? What is it remembered for?
items.d: Famous for a collapse? A world record? A last-over finish? A single over? Know the headline before you watch.

items.q: What was at stake? Group stage, final, series decider, survival?
items.d: The context of what was on the line determines every decision you'll see. A dead rubber is played differently.

items.q: Who are the two teams and what was their rivalry like at this time?
items.d: India vs Pakistan carries entirely different weight than, say, a bilateral ODI series between two mid-table sides.

sections.title: Know the Players Before You See Them Play

items.q: Who are the star players on each side for this era?
items.d: Sachin Tendulkar in 2003 is different from Sachin in 1996. Know roughly where each player is in their career arc.

items.q: Is anyone playing their last match, debut, or milestone innings?
items.d: Watching Tendulkar's 100th international century knowing it's coming changes your entire focus. Check for milestones.

items.q: Who was considered the best bowler in the world at this time?
items.d: Context of bowling greatness changes how you read dismissals. Being dismissed by Warne in 1999 is not average.

items.q: Are any players in this match now legends whose careers you can trace?
items.d: Watching a 22-year-old Virat Kohli in 2011 knowing what he becomes — that is a uniquely historical pleasure.

items.q: What team balance did each side have — batting-heavy, bowling-heavy, balanced?
items.d: A team with 7 batters says something about how they planned to play. A 5-bowler team says the opposite.

sections.title: Understand the Conditions of That Day

items.q: What was the venue? What were its characteristics in that era?
items.d: Lord's in 1981 = green seaming pitch. Wankhede in 2011 = flat batting pitch with evening dew. Venue = half the story.

items.q: What were the pitch and weather conditions on that specific day?
items.d: Old match commentary and Wikipedia match summaries usually mention this. 10 minutes of reading, massive payoff.

items.q: What were the playing conditions and rules of that era?
items.d: Fielding restrictions changed over decades. Old white ball rules differ. Field placement rules evolved. Context matters.

items.q: Day match or under lights? What time of day did play begin?
items.d: Evening dew, morning swing, afternoon heat — these are as important in historical matches as in live ones.

sections.title: Set Your Mindset — The Historical Watcher's Pledge

items.q: You likely know the result. Commit to watching for HOW, not WHAT.
items.d: Knowing India won the 2011 final doesn't spoil it — it lets you watch every Dhoni decision knowing its weight.

items.q: Resist skipping ahead. Every phase has meaning in hindsight.
items.d: The boring passage of play before a wicket — that's the pressure build-up. The best watching is slow watching.

items.q: Form your own view before reading Wikipedia or commentary reviews.
items.d: Watch first, read analysis second. If you read it first you are borrowing someone else's understanding, not building yours.

items.q: Pause and predict at key moments — then see what actually happened.
items.d: Over 48 of a Test. A spinner brought on in over 7 of a T20 chase. Pause, predict, then unpause. This is active watching.

phases.name: Part 2 of 3
phases.title: While You Watch
phases.sub: You know the result. So watch for decisions, patterns, and the why — not just the what.

items.q: [TEST] Watch the session, not just the over. How are runs accumulated?
items.d: A session of 80/0 followed by a session of 50/5 is the story of Test cricket. Patience then collapse. Watch the arc.

items.q: [TEST] Who is building the innings — the anchors vs the stroke-makers?
items.d: Test cricket is about partnerships and patience. A 200-run partnership over 4 hours looks boring — it is actually dominance.

items.q: [TEST] Watch how spinners bowl in their own conditions over time.
items.d: A spinner in over 8 on day 1 vs over 8 on day 4 is bowling on a completely different pitch. Note how their figures change.

items.q: [ODI] Watch the powerplay, consolidation, and slog overs as three separate battles.
items.d: Classic ODI structure: aggressive PP → anchor build → slog overs explosion. Who won each phase?

items.q: [T20] Every over is an event. Watch the captaincy — bowling changes, field settings.
items.d: T20 captaincy is chess at 100km/h. A misfield, a wrong bowling choice at the death — these are match-defining moments.

items.q: [T20] Identify the momentum-shift over. Almost every T20 has one over that decides it.
items.d: It's usually an over where 4+ wickets are taken, or 20+ runs are scored. Find it — that's the match in microcosm.

sections.title: Watch the Battles — Batter vs Bowler

items.q: Pick one key batter. Watch their footwork, head position, and shot selection.
items.d: Great batters keep their head still, move their feet to the pitch of the ball, and play late. You'll see this in legends.

items.q: Pick one key bowler. Watch their run-up, release point, and field placement.
items.d: A bowler who sets the field before delivering is thinking two balls ahead. This is what separates great from average.

items.q: Watch how the batter handles the bowler's best delivery — the one they're famous for.
items.d: Warne's flipper. McGrath's away-swinger. Muralitharan's doosra. When it's bowled, pause: did you see the grip? The release?

items.q: Find the over where the match's best batter faces the match's best bowler.
items.d: This is cricket's gladiatorial moment. The battle of equals. Note who blinks first and exactly how it happens.

items.q: Watch for verbal/physical confrontations — how are they handled?
items.d: Sledging, staring-downs, send-offs. Historical context makes these more interesting — you can look up what was said after.

items.q: When does the captain make a bowling change? What was the trigger?
items.d: A captain who changes after 1 expensive over is reactive. One who waits 3 overs for the plan to work is patient. Both can be right.

items.q: How is the field set for each batter? Does it change between batters?
items.d: A radically different field for two batters in the same over tells you the captain has very specific individual plans.

items.q: Is the batting captain sending in a nightwatchman? Why or why not? (Test)
items.d: A nightwatchman at 5:30pm on Day 2 = captain protecting a key batter for tomorrow. A small decision, huge implications.

items.q: Watch the captain under pressure — does decision-making deteriorate?
items.d: Captains who panic make bowling changes too quickly, move fielders reactively, lose the plot. It's visible.

items.q: Are there any obviously wrong decisions in hindsight? Note them.
items.d: A dropped catch at 23, player X should have bowled over 47 — these are things you can now see clearly. Write them.

items.q: When does momentum first shift — and what caused it?
items.d: Momentum shift = the moment the crowd changes, the fielding team's body language lifts, the batting team tenses.

items.q: Identify the passage of play that decided the match.
items.d: Not the final ball — the passage 30 minutes before where the match became un-loseable or unwinnable for one team.

items.q: Watch the players' body language during pressure moments.
items.d: A fielder dropping their head. A bowler celebrating too hard. A batter talking to themselves. Human detail, historical context.

items.q: If watching a famous finish — pause just before the last 10 minutes.
items.d: Ask yourself: at this point, who do I think wins? Then unpause. Experiencing the uncertainty is what all historical viewing is about.

phases.name: Part 3 of 3
phases.title: After You Watch
phases.sub: Read the history. Compare your view. Build permanent cricket knowledge from what you just witnessed.

sections.title: Compare Your Read Against the Historical Record

items.q: Read the Wikipedia match summary — what did you miss?
items.d: Wikipedia pages for famous matches are usually detailed. Read after watching to find what you didn't notice live.

items.q: Find post-match commentary or video analysis — what did experts say?
items.d: YouTube has post-match analysis for famous matches going back decades. ESPNcricinfo has written analysis. Use them.

items.q: Did you correctly identify the turning point while watching?
items.d: Compare your turning point with what experts named. If they differ, who was right — and why? This is productive disagreement.

items.q: Were your player assessments accurate vs their historical reputation?
items.d: You saw a bowler get hit for 60 in 8 overs. But they're considered great. What context are you missing?

sections.title: Place the Match in Cricket History

items.q: What did this match change — in the series, in careers, in cricket history?
items.d: The 2001 Eden Gardens Test revived India's Test culture. The 1992 World Cup final legitimised Pakistan as world champions.

items.q: Did this match launch or end a career? Any turning-point player story?
items.d: VVS Laxman's 281 in 2001 saved his career. Ponting's 140* in the 2003 World Cup final cemented his legacy. Look for these.

items.q: Was this match part of a series? What happened before and after?
items.d: A single match in a 5-Test series has its own internal logic. Understanding what came before changes everything.

items.q: What records were set or broken in this match?
items.d: Highest chase ever. Largest margin of defeat. Most wickets in a session. Records are data points for calibrating greatness.

items.q: How was this match covered at the time? What did people think then vs now?
items.d: Sometimes matches dismissed as upsets are now seen as masterclasses. The gap between contemporary and historical view is interesting.

items.q: Write 3 sentences describing what this match was and why it was special.
items.d: Forcing yourself to articulate it in 3 sentences means you now permanently own this match's story in your memory.

items.q: Add this match to your mental map: format, era, teams, why famous.
items.d: Build a simple running list. 10 famous matches studied = a working cricket library. 50 = encyclopaedic knowledge.

items.q: Identify one player from this match to read more about.
items.d: Every great match has a player whose story is richer than a single innings. Follow one thread after each match.

items.q: What question did this match raise that you still don't fully understand?
items.d: Did you see a dismissal and not understand the law? A field setting you couldn't explain? Carry this forward.

items.q: Can you explain this match to someone in 60 seconds?
items.d: Teams. Stakes. What happened. Turning point. Result. Why it matters. If you can do this fluently, you understood it.

items.q: Can you name the player of the match and explain specifically why?
items.d: Not just 'he scored 141' but 'he scored 141 on a pitch assisting pace in a high-pressure final against the world's best attack.'

items.q: If you could ask one player one question about this match, what would it be?
items.d: This question reveals where your understanding is deepest and where the real gaps are. It's a diagnostic.

items.q: Would you recommend this match to another cricket learner? How would you describe it?
items.d: Teaching consolidates learning faster than anything else. If you can recommend it with context, you've truly internalized it.
